merges.merged_rewrite_direction: Open chapter 9 on Kem's first bad stitch (line 3825) without re-quoting Vel's suturing instructions from Ch8 line 3804. merges.merged_rewrite_direction: At Ch14 line 6295, drop the handwriting observation and enter the journal through what Kem is reading or what information Vel is extracting. This is the 4th description of contrasting handwritings in the journal. Let the clue planted in Ch12 stand, let Ch13 use the journal without restating it, and let Ch14 turn it directly into new information or leverage rather than another visual re-introduction of the same physical detail.

merges.merged_rewrite_direction: Give chapter 14 a different dominant shape from chapter 13. Cut or compress one labor block, bring the altered alliance terms or basin-specific pressure to the front, and avoid restaging the full collapse-rescue-campfire grammar chapter 13 just used. A cold reader should be able to summarize chapter 13 as the betrayal-confrontation chapter and chapter 14 as the explicit-terms/choice chapter, not describe both primarily as consecutive collapsing-settlement rescues.

merges.merged_rewrite_direction: Open Ch16 with a brief time-and-position anchor, then move immediately to the new information: Dren packing, the relay flashes, the riders. Cut or compress the logistics paragraph at line 6899 that recaps Ch15's route arithmetic. merges.reason: All three findings flag the same Ch16 opening passage recapping Ch15's travel logistics. R15 is the most specific, identifying an 8-word near-exact repeated phrase. LW07-003 and LW08-001 describe the identical problem from overlapping local windows. One combined directive is cleaner than three findings targeting the same opening paragraph.

merges.merged_rewrite_direction: Cut or compress the repeated basin orientation at the top of Chapter 18 and open on the first materially new tactical development: a worsened injury state, a forced spend, a visibly narrowing lane, or Sable's approach becoming actionable. The reader carries the basin rules, Osen's containment method, and the eight-hundred-meter geography from Chapter 17. Do not restate them.
merges.reason: Both findings target the Ch17/18 boundary. LW08-004 says Ch18 replays the same trapped-under-cover dynamic. LW09-001 says the orientation is repeated almost verbatim. canonical_choices.grounding: Ch7 line 2911 manuscript establishment. Corroborated by the continuity sheet spend trail: 300 (birth) -> 288 (Ch2 departure waste) -> 286 (pre-Ch4) -> 245 (Ch4 combat) -> 225 (Ch7 Spent Towns entry). The number 166 at Ch11 line 4973 does not correspond to any tracked spending event.

Continuity sheet: Vel 'Drops to 18 in the Margins while Kem burns through the reserve she meant for the Scour' (Ch12 state transition). This requires two on-page spends in Ch12 from her Ch10 exit level of 20. Currently only one spend (footbridge reinforcement, 1 level) is visible. The missing second 1-level spend must be surfaced in Ch12, not resolved by changing Ch13's number to 19.

consistency_directives.rule: Do not open a chapter by restating information the reader received at the end of the previous chapter. Each chapter must begin with forward motion, a new development, or at most a single brief orienting clause before advancing. Travel summaries, logistics recaps, and re-explanations of the prior chapter's final state are revision targets, not opening strategies.
consistency_directives.reason: Eight findings across six chapter boundaries flag the same drafting-model tendency: opening a new chapter by re-briefing the prior chapter's closing state. The pattern appears at Ch8/9 (suturing recap), Ch11/12 (distrust question restated), Ch15/16 (route arithmetic re-explained), and Ch17/18 (basin orientation repeated). This is a systemic tendency that likely affects chapter openings not explicitly flagged.

consistency_directives.rule: Each chapter should contain no more than 20 hand/palm/finger references. Keep only load-bearing instances where hands perform a specific, non-substitutable action (holding a specific object, performing a skill, a hand-specific injury). Replace atmospheric and habitual hand references with varied body parts: posture, jaw, shoulders, feet, weight shift, breathing, gaze. Do not replace hand references with another single substitute that creates a new tic.
consistency_directives.reason: Four chapters were flagged (Ch5: 51, Ch8: 44, Ch13: 42, Ch19: 44) but the manuscript average is 32.4 per chapter across all 19 chapters, indicating a pervasive drafting tendency to default to hands for every physical beat, emotional registration, and moment of hesitation. Unflagged chapters likely carry the same inflation.

consistency_directives.rule: Retain at most three 'the shape of [abstract noun]' constructions across the entire manuscript. Specifically retain: (1) Ch13 line 5564's contrastive pair ('Not the shape of a hope. The shape of a requirement.') which earns its repetition through deliberate parallelism, and (2) Ch18 line 8602's 'The shape of the world started to slip' which may be defensible as literal threshold description. Replace all other instances with constructions native to their scene: physical metaphors, Ledger-vocabulary substitutions, or direct statement. No replacement phrase should introduce a new recurring template.
consistency_directives.reason: Eleven instances across seven chapters. By the late book the formula is audible as a drafting default rather than a chosen figure. The global cap forces each chapter's revision agent to replace its local instances while the consistency directive preserves the two to three uses where the formula does genuine rhetorical work.

consistency_directives.rule: Do not re-describe the physical appearance of a character, object, or location that has already been established in an earlier chapter, unless the re-description serves a genuinely new narrative function: a materially changed physical state (new injury, visible deterioration), a unique institutional perspective that reframes the detail, or a plot-driving reaction to the detail. On reappearance, use a brief reference or a single new observation rather than a full visual re-inventory. Trust the reader's memory across chapter boundaries.
consistency_directives.reason: Twelve findings across nine chapters flag the same drafting-model tendency: re-introducing known visual details each time a character, object, or location reappears. Kem's height is re-described three times, the signet is re-inventoried with near-verbatim phrasing, Dren's scar gets four full descriptions after the reader watched every stitch, the journal handwriting appears six or seven times, Dren's Mira motivation is restated six times, and the settlement-arrival template repeats its Shaping-seam architecture assessment. This is a pervasive tendency that likely affects every chapter where a known element reappears.

context_injections.cross_chapter_context: Ch2 shows Kem's actual departure from the Vault: he uses an ability at the gate, blows stone loose in the gate hall, walks through the dust, and heads into the wild. Breck's yard is not on Kem's actual route in Ch2. It functions as a courier relay point on the rumor chain, not a confirmed physical sighting. Sorren's answer in Ch1 must preserve this distinction.

Ch1 already establishes for the reader: Kem used an ability at the gate, blew stone loose in the gate hall, walked through the dust. House Aldraste. The son. First ability use in his life. Ch2's revision agent should not replay these same departure beats. Enter Ch2 after this establishment or find a different angle that adds net-new story information.

By the end of Ch1, the reader already knows from Vel's interiority: zero is passage at the Scour, a cascade needs two people spending down together to hold the door open, and Vel's side of the equation is already thin. Ch3's negotiation must pivot to genuinely new leverage beyond this already-disclosed math — what Vel will manipulate, what she owes the person who crossed, what Kem can infer about the cost of trusting her.

Kem's canonical level trail for the retrospective: 225 at Spent Towns entry (Ch7 line 2911), 183 after the silo fight (Ch8), 160 after Thessa's settlement escape (Ch10, losing 23 levels on defensive abilities), then additional incidental spending between Ch10 and Ch12. The Ch11 retrospective at line 4973 must use these correct numbers. The sequence '166 -> 160 -> 148' is wrong at every anchor point.

Ch12 opens with 'Two days after the river' as its first temporal marker. Any fix to Ch11's timeline arithmetic — whether changing '5 days since Thessa's kitchen' to 3 or inserting an explicit 2-day travel skip — must ensure Ch12's 'two days after the river' still lands on a coherent calendar when read consecutively.

Vel enters Ch12 at level 20 and should exit at 18 per the continuity sheet. Only one on-page spend (footbridge reinforcement, 1 level) currently accounts for part of this 2-level drop. When adding causally necessary scene material for the word count, surface a second 1-level Vel spend to close the continuity gap before Ch13 begins treating her as 18.

Vel drops to 20 in Ch10. Ch12 currently shows one visible spend (footbridge reinforcement, 1 level, making her 19 on-page). The canonical level at Ch13 entry is 18 per the continuity sheet, requiring a second 1-level spend in Ch12. The Ch12 revision agent has been directed to surface this spend via the length_chapter_12_min_words finding. If the Ch12 revision confirms two on-page spends from 20 to 18, Ch13 references at 18 are correct. If only one spend remains visible, adjust Ch13 references to match the reader-visible trail.

Ch17 establishes the Scour garrison as four opponents: Commander Osen (permanent garrison commander who has guarded the site for five years), two freshly deployed descent agents (sent for the Kem retrieval), and one circuiting specialist. Ch18 must use this same four-person roster with consistent role labels throughout the basin fight. Do not introduce additional combatants or change role designations at the chapter boundary.
